heroOverview: Syngamiasis is a rare parasitic infection of the respiratory tract caused by the nematode Syngamus laryngeus, a worm normally found in the airways of birds that can accidentally infect humans. Human cases number fewer than 100 in the published literature and occur predominantly in residents of or travellers to rural areas of the Caribbean, South America, and parts of Asia where infected birds share close proximity with people. The hallmark presentation is a persistent, irritating cough sometimes accompanied by the sensation of something moving in the throat or larynx. Unlike many helminth infections, syngamiasis localises almost exclusively to the upper airways rather than the intestine, making it diagnostically unusual. Most cases resolve after endoscopic or anthelmintic removal of the adult worm.

keyStats.prevalence: Fewer than 100 human cases documented in world literature; considered extremely rare (WHO, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Cases concentrated in Caribbean islands (Trinidad, Martinique), Brazil, Philippines, and parts of Southeast Asia
keyStats.avgAge: No dominant age group; children and adults equally affected when exposure occurs
keyStats.globalCases: True global burden unknown due to rarity; likely under-diagnosed in endemic regions

definition: Syngamiasis (ICD-10: B83.3) is a zoonotic helminthiasis caused by the nematode Syngamus laryngeus (occasionally Mammomonogamus laryngeus), a member of the family Syngamidae. The parasite normally inhabits the trachea and bronchi of birds, but can infect mammals, including humans, when infective larvae are ingested through contaminated food or water, or through skin contact with infected soil. Adult worms are small — females reach 10–18 mm, males 4–6 mm — and they attach in copula to the mucosal lining of the larynx, trachea, or bronchi, where they provoke a local inflammatory and mechanical irritation. The infection falls under infectious disease and, given its respiratory location, is frequently first encountered by pulmonologists or ENT specialists unfamiliar with it. Eosinophilia is often present. Because human infection is incidental and the worm cannot complete its life cycle in a human host, adult worms do not reproduce in the human respiratory tract, and reinfection requires fresh exposure. The infection is also called mammomonogamiasis after the alternative genus name used in some classification systems.

typesClassification.description: The most common human form; adult worms attach to the laryngeal mucosa and produce a persistent cough with tickling and foreign-body sensation in the throat.
Less common; worms are located lower in the trachea or bronchi, sometimes causing mild dyspnoea or wheeze in addition to cough.
Occasionally worms are found incidentally during endoscopy performed for other reasons, with minimal or no symptoms reported.

primarySymptoms: Persistent dry or irritating cough that does not respond to standard cough remedies and has lasted weeks to months — often the reason the patient seeks medical attention.
A sensation of something crawling, moving, or tickling in the throat, larynx, or upper chest that prompts repeated throat-clearing.
Foreign-body sensation in the pharynx or larynx, frequently described as a thread or small object that cannot be swallowed or coughed out.
Intermittent haemoptysis (blood-tinged sputum) resulting from local mucosal trauma caused by the worm's attachment by buccal capsule to the airway lining.
Throat irritation and rawness, particularly felt during or immediately after coughing episodes.
Occasional mild dyspnoea or sensation of incomplete breath with particularly large worm burdens or in young children with smaller airways.
Episodic mild wheeze audible to the patient themselves, usually without the airflow obstruction measurable on spirometry.
Expulsion of a small reddish-brown worm pair — the coupled male and female — either coughed up or visible at the back of the throat, which is simultaneously diagnostic and alarming to the patient.
Mild systemic eosinophilia detected incidentally on a blood count, reflecting the immune response to the migrating and resident worm.
Nausea or gagging triggered by the foreign-body sensation, particularly when the worm is located in the hypopharynx.
earlyWarningSigns: A new tickling sensation deep in the throat that does not respond to lozenges or fluids.
A cough that begins suddenly in someone recently returned from a rural endemic area.
Faint blood streaks in sputum without an obvious upper respiratory infection.
Sensation of intermittent movement in the larynx during quiet breathing.
emergencySigns: Severe respiratory distress or stridor in a young child, where a worm in a narrow larynx or trachea can cause near-obstruction.
High fever with worsening dyspnoea, which may indicate secondary bacterial bronchitis superimposed on the worm infestation.

causes.cause: Ingestion of infective larvae via contaminated food or water
causes.description: The main route of human infection is swallowing third-stage larvae present in vegetables, water, or soil in endemic areas; the larvae then migrate to the respiratory tract via the gut wall or bloodstream to reach their final site in the larynx or trachea.

causes.cause: Direct skin penetration in heavily contaminated soil
causes.description: Infective larvae may penetrate intact skin in agricultural workers walking barefoot or working in fields contaminated with bird droppings, after which they undergo a tissue migration reaching the respiratory mucosa.

causes.cause: Inhalation of larvae-contaminated dust or aerosols
causes.description: In environments with heavy bird populations and dry conditions, lightweight larvae may become airborne and enter the respiratory tract directly, bypassing the gastrointestinal route and producing an even more direct laryngeal infection.

Domestic fowl, turkeys, pheasants, and wild birds in endemic regions harbour adult Syngamus worms in their airways and shed eggs in secretions; people who slaughter, clean, or handle infected birds can inadvertently ingest or inhale larvae.

causes.cause: Consumption of undercooked or raw poultry
causes.description: Eating inadequately cooked chicken, turkey, or game birds in regions where the parasite is endemic can transmit encysted larvae that survive mild heating and later migrate to the human airway.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to Caribbean or South American endemic areas
riskFactors.description: Trinidad, Martinique, Brazil, and parts of the Philippines account for the majority of reported human cases; travellers to rural agricultural communities in these regions carry the greatest exposure risk.

riskFactors.factor: Close contact with domestic poultry or birds
riskFactors.description: Farmers, poultry workers, and people raising chickens, turkeys, or ducks in endemic areas face repeated exposure to Syngamus-infected birds and their contaminated environments.

riskFactors.factor: Consumption of raw or undercooked poultry
riskFactors.description: Cultural food practices involving lightly cooked or raw chicken or game birds in endemic areas increase the likelihood of ingesting viable larvae not killed by adequate heat.

Agricultural labourers or children playing barefoot in soil heavily contaminated by infected bird droppings risk transcutaneous larval penetration, particularly in warm, moist climates that favour larval survival.
Although few data exist, immunocompromised individuals may experience a more pronounced local inflammatory response or atypical presentation, and diagnosis may be further delayed in this group.

Children with narrower laryngeal and tracheal diameters may develop more pronounced symptoms — including partial airway obstruction — from even a single worm pair than adults with the same worm burden.

affectedDemographics: Rural agricultural workers in the Caribbean, particularly Trinidad and Tobago and Martinique
Residents of endemic rural Brazil and northern South America
Travellers returning from endemic areas who consumed local food or had contact with poultry
Children in endemic regions, in whom symptoms may be more severe due to smaller airways
Workers in backyard poultry operations in endemic settings
diagnosisOverview: Syngamiasis is almost invariably missed on first presentation because clinicians in non-endemic countries have rarely or never seen a case, and the symptoms — chronic cough, foreign-body sensation, and occasional haemoptysis — match dozens of common respiratory conditions. A travel history to a Caribbean or South American endemic area, combined with a cough that is unusually prolonged and associated with a sensation of movement in the throat, should raise suspicion. The definitive diagnostic step in most published cases is direct laryngoscopy or bronchoscopy, during which the red or brownish worm pair, attached in permanent copula (the Y-shape formed by the smaller male attached to the female), is visualised and removed. Eosinophilia on a full blood count is a non-specific but supportive finding, present in roughly 50–70% of reported cases. Stool examination for eggs is unreliable because adult worms in the human respiratory tract do not consistently shed eggs in detectable quantities. Chest X-ray and CT are usually normal or show only minor mucosal thickening. Molecular identification (PCR or mitochondrial sequencing) of the removed worm confirms the species when histology is inconclusive. The key diagnostic challenge is ensuring that a patient in whom a worm is unexpectedly visualised endoscopically is not simply discharged with the worm in situ.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualises the adult worm attached to the laryngeal or pharyngeal mucosa, allowing simultaneous diagnosis and removal.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A flexible or rigid endoscope is passed through the nose or mouth while you are awake or under light sedation; the procedure takes 10–20 minutes and the worm, if present, is removed with biopsy forceps.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Examines the trachea and bronchi when laryngoscopy is unrevealing or when worms are suspected lower in the airway; also the therapeutic removal tool in tracheal or bronchial cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under local anaesthesia or conscious sedation; a flexible bronchoscope is passed through the mouth or nose and advanced to the level of the worm.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects eosinophilia, which is present in roughly half of cases and supports a parasitic aetiology for unexplained respiratory symptoms.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw from the arm; results available within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Sought as a rapid non-invasive test, though its sensitivity for syngamiasis is low because worms in the human host do not reliably produce detectable eggs.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You provide a stool sample; laboratory analysis takes 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes more common causes of haemoptysis and persistent cough such as tuberculosis, tumour, or bronchiectasis, and occasionally reveals airway thickening over the worm site.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard imaging; CT involves a brief scan with the patient lying still for a few minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Histopathology and molecular identification of removed worm
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms species identity (Syngamus laryngeus vs. other nematodes) when macroscopic appearance is ambiguous.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The removed worm is fixed in formalin and sent to a laboratory; results take several days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of syngamiasis centres on physical removal of the adult worm from the airway, typically performed at the time of diagnostic endoscopy. Because the worm is adherent to the mucosa by its buccal capsule, gentle traction with biopsy or grasping forceps under direct vision is needed; fragmentation of the worm during removal should be avoided to prevent leaving remnants that continue to irritate. Most patients experience complete resolution of symptoms within 24–48 hours of worm removal. Anthelmintic drug therapy is used as an adjunct or, in cases where endoscopy is not immediately available, as definitive treatment. Thiabendazole was historically the first-line anthelmintic, but it has largely been replaced by albendazole or ivermectin, which have better tolerability and established activity against Syngamus species. A standard course of albendazole (400 mg twice daily for 3–5 days) or ivermectin (200 mcg/kg as a single or double dose) is generally well tolerated and achieves parasitological cure in the majority of cases when endoscopic removal is not possible. Because human infection is a dead-end for the parasite, repeat treatment is rarely needed unless fresh exposure occurs. There are no approved vaccines or immune prophylactics, and prevention relies entirely on avoiding contaminated food, water, and environmental exposure.

medicalTreatments.name: Endoscopic worm removal (laryngoscopy/bronchoscopy)
medicalTreatments.description: The definitive treatment in virtually all reported cases; the worm pair is grasped and extracted under direct vision at laryngoscopy or bronchoscopy, providing both diagnosis and cure in a single procedure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Success in approximately 95% of reported cases when the worm is visualised; symptoms resolve within 24–48 hours of removal in nearly all patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole
medicalTreatments.description: A broad-spectrum benzimidazole anthelmintic given orally at 400 mg twice daily for 3–5 days; used when endoscopy is unavailable, as adjunctive therapy after partial removal, or when multiple worms are suspected.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Published case reports show parasitological cure in the majority of patients, with symptom resolution typically within one week of completing the course.

medicalTreatments.name: Ivermectin
medicalTreatments.description: A macrocyclic lactone anthelmintic effective against Syngamus; given at 200 mcg/kg orally, usually as a single dose repeated after two weeks, and is the preferred agent in settings where albendazole is unavailable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Limited case data but several published reports describe complete worm expulsion or symptomatic cure within 1–2 weeks; at least 1 comparative case series reports 80% or greater success rates.

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroids (short course)
medicalTreatments.description: Occasionally used as an adjunct when significant mucosal inflammation or partial airway oedema accompanies the infection, particularly in young children; not a primary treatment but may reduce bronchospasm.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Supportive role only; direct antiparasitic effect is absent, but mucosal inflammation typically improves within 48–72 hours of a short course.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mebendazole
alternativeTreatments.description: An alternative benzimidazole mentioned in a small number of older case reports; less commonly used today because albendazole has superior bioavailability and is better studied.

alternativeTreatments.name: Thiabendazole
alternativeTreatments.description: The original anthelmintic used in early syngamiasis cases; now largely replaced due to a more pronounced side-effect profile, but still mentioned in older literature and may be available in some endemic-area formularies.

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease specialist or pulmonologist should be involved in any case of unexplained chronic cough with eosinophilia, particularly in travellers from endemic areas. An ENT or interventional pulmonology specialist is needed to perform the definitive endoscopic removal. Primary care physicians unfamiliar with the parasite often pursue multiple rounds of antibiotics or antitussives without benefit before the diagnosis is considered.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an infectious disease specialist with experience in tropical parasitic infections, ideally in a centre with expertise in returned travellers or imported infections. An ENT surgeon or interventional pulmonologist with the capability to perform awake nasopharyngoscopy and flexible bronchoscopy at the same visit is the most efficient route to diagnosis and treatment. Ask whether the centre has submitted or reviewed cases of imported helminth infections.
preventionStrategies: Thoroughly cook all poultry and game birds to an internal temperature of at least 74°C, which kills Syngamus larvae.
Avoid drinking untreated water or eating unwashed raw vegetables in endemic rural areas of the Caribbean and South America.
Wear footwear in fields or soil areas heavily contaminated by bird droppings to prevent transcutaneous larval penetration.
Use insect- and bird-proof food storage to prevent contamination of produce by infected avian faeces.
Educate farm workers in endemic areas about the risk and the characteristic symptom pattern so early medical consultation is sought.
Control infections in domestic poultry flocks with veterinary anthelmintics where feasible to reduce environmental larval contamination.
lifestyleModifications: If diagnosed, avoid activities that involve prolonged inhalation of dust from bird enclosures until treatment is complete.
Wear a protective mask when cleaning poultry housing or handling large numbers of birds in endemic areas.
Report the travel history and bird-contact history explicitly to every clinician involved, so that the diagnosis is considered.
After treatment, follow up with a clinician at two to four weeks to confirm resolution of symptoms and eosinophilia.

recommended: Well-cooked poultry and game birds at temperatures that ensure larval killing
Bottled or treated water when travelling in endemic regions
Washed and peeled raw vegetables rather than unwashed produce in endemic settings
avoid: Raw or lightly cooked poultry in rural Caribbean or South American settings
Untreated stream, pond, or well water in endemic areas
Unwashed raw vegetables grown in soil contaminated by bird droppings

exerciseGuidelines: No specific exercise restriction is required unless the worm burden is large enough to cause partial airway obstruction with exertion, in which case vigorous exercise should be deferred until after treatment. Most patients with syngamiasis do not have significant airflow limitation and can maintain their usual activity level after endoscopic removal.
dailyManagement: Complete the full course of any prescribed anthelmintic even if symptoms start to improve early.
Keep a note of symptom onset date and travel history to assist the clinician in identifying the source.
Avoid re-exposure to suspected contaminated environments until treatment is confirmed successful.
Return for follow-up bronchoscopy if cough or foreign-body sensation does not resolve within two weeks of initial treatment.
prognosis: The prognosis for syngamiasis is excellent. The condition is self-limiting in the sense that the worm cannot reproduce in the human host, so a single successful removal or anthelmintic course is curative. In virtually every case in the published literature where the diagnosis was made and the worm removed, patients recovered completely without residual respiratory impairment. Delayed diagnosis is the main clinical hazard, leading to months of unnecessary symptom burden and repeated ineffective antibiotic or antitussive treatments. No deaths attributable directly to syngamiasis have been reported in the human medical literature. The rare case complicated by secondary airway inflammation or obstruction in a young child resolves with appropriate management. Long-term follow-up is not required unless re-exposure in an endemic area occurs.
recoveryTimeline: Symptom relief typically begins within hours of worm removal and is complete within 24 to 48 hours for most patients. When anthelmintic drug therapy is used as the sole treatment, symptom resolution is slower, with most patients reporting improvement within three to seven days of starting the course. Eosinophilia, if present, normalises over two to four weeks after successful treatment. No prolonged convalescence is needed.
complications: Partial airway obstruction in young children, where a worm in the narrow laryngeal lumen can cause audible stridor and mild respiratory distress.
Secondary bacterial bronchitis at the worm attachment site, presenting as purulent cough and low-grade fever superimposed on the parasitic infection.
Prolonged diagnostic delay resulting in months of unnecessary antibiotics, antitussives, and investigations.
Mucosal trauma and localised bleeding from repeated coughing episodes before the worm is identified and removed.

sources.title: Nosanchuk JS et al. Mammomonogamus laryngeus infection in humans: a case report and review. Clinical Infectious Diseases, 1995

confusedWithConditions.name: Bronchitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bronchitis produces cough with sputum production and usually follows a viral upper respiratory infection; syngamiasis causes a dry tickling cough with foreign-body sensation and no productive phase, and bronchitis shows no visible worm on laryngoscopy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eosinophilic oesophagitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eosinophilic oesophagitis causes dysphagia and chest pain due to oesophageal eosinophilic infiltration and is unrelated to parasitic airway infestation; both may involve eosinophilia and a sensation in the throat.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pneumonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pneumonia produces fever, productive cough, and consolidation on chest imaging, while syngamiasis produces a clear chest X-ray and a chronic tickling cough without systemic illness.

relatedConditions.name: Visceral larva migrans
relatedConditions.relevance: Another zoonotic nematode infection in which larval migration through human tissue causes eosinophilia and organ injury, sharing the concept of incidental human infection by animal parasites.

relatedConditions.name: Other specified helminthiases
relatedConditions.relevance: The broader ICD-10 category that captures rare human nematode infections with similar epidemiological and diagnostic challenges to syngamiasis.

relatedConditions.name: Mixed intestinal helminthiases
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-infection with multiple helminths is common in endemic settings where syngamiasis occurs, and patients may simultaneously carry intestinal worms.

relatedConditions.name: Filariasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A filarial nematode infection also causing eosinophilia and systemic symptoms from tissue migration of parasitic worms, sharing the endemic geographic overlap with syngamiasis.

faqs.question: What is syngamiasis?
faqs.answer: Syngamiasis is a rare respiratory infection caused by the worm Syngamus laryngeus, normally a parasite of birds, that accidentally infects humans. The worm lodges in the larynx or trachea and causes persistent cough and a sensation of movement in the throat.

faqs.question: How do you get syngamiasis?
faqs.answer: Most people become infected by swallowing infective larvae in contaminated food, water, or undercooked poultry in endemic areas. Less commonly, larvae penetrate the skin or are inhaled from dust in environments heavily contaminated by infected bird droppings.

faqs.question: Where in the world does syngamiasis occur?
faqs.answer: Almost all reported human cases come from the Caribbean — especially Trinidad and Martinique — northern South America, the Philippines, and parts of Southeast Asia. It is extremely rare in Europe or North America except in returned travellers.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of syngamiasis?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are a persistent irritating cough, a sensation of movement or a foreign body in the throat or larynx, occasional blood-streaked sputum, and throat irritation. Some patients actually cough up the small red-brown worm pair.

faqs.question: How is syngamiasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made by laryngoscopy or bronchoscopy, which allows the clinician to see and remove the worm. Blood tests often show eosinophilia. Chest X-ray is usually normal. Stool testing is unreliable because the worm in humans does not consistently shed detectable eggs.

faqs.question: How is syngamiasis treated?
faqs.answer: The most effective treatment is endoscopic removal of the worm under direct vision during laryngoscopy or bronchoscopy. Anthelmintic drugs — albendazole or ivermectin — are used when endoscopy is unavailable or as adjuncts. Most patients recover completely within 24 to 48 hours of removal.

faqs.question: Can syngamiasis be cured?
faqs.answer: Yes. Syngamiasis is fully curable. Because the worm cannot reproduce in the human body, a single successful endoscopic removal or anthelmintic course eliminates the infection. No cases of untreatable syngamiasis have been reported.

faqs.question: Is syngamiasis dangerous?
faqs.answer: In most adults it is not life-threatening but causes significant discomfort. In young children the worm can partially block the narrow larynx and cause respiratory distress, which is the main serious complication. The condition is fully curable when diagnosed.

faqs.question: Can you spread syngamiasis to another person?
faqs.answer: No. Syngamiasis is not contagious between people. The infection requires ingestion or contact with larvae from infected birds or contaminated environments. The worm cannot complete its life cycle in humans and therefore does not produce infectious eggs.

faqs.question: What does the syngamiasis worm look like?
faqs.answer: The adult worms are small — the female is 10 to 18 mm and the male is 4 to 6 mm — and they are permanently attached together in a Y-shaped pair. They are reddish-brown in colour due to the blood they ingest. When coughed up or removed, they are occasionally mistaken for a blood clot or piece of tissue.

faqs.question: How long do symptoms of syngamiasis last without treatment?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, symptoms can persist for months to years, since the worm can live in the human respiratory tract for a prolonged period. The persistent cough and foreign-body sensation do not resolve spontaneously unless the worm is expelled or dies naturally.

faqs.question: Why is syngamiasis so often misdiagnosed?
faqs.answer: Clinicians in non-endemic countries rarely encounter the infection and are not aware of it. The symptoms — chronic cough, foreign-body sensation, and occasional haemoptysis — mimic far more common conditions such as bronchitis, reflux, or laryngopharyngeal irritation, leading to repeated antibiotic courses before the parasitic cause is considered.

faqs.question: Do I need a bronchoscopy to diagnose syngamiasis?
faqs.answer: In most cases, yes. Laryngoscopy or bronchoscopy is both the diagnostic and the therapeutic step. Blood and stool tests cannot reliably confirm the diagnosis. If the travel and symptom history is suggestive, asking specifically for an endoscopic examination is the fastest path to diagnosis and cure.

faqs.question: Which antibiotic treats syngamiasis?
faqs.answer: Antibiotics do not treat syngamiasis because it is a worm infection, not a bacterial one. The correct agents are antiparasitic drugs: albendazole (400 mg twice daily for 3–5 days) or ivermectin (200 mcg/kg). Endoscopic removal remains the most effective treatment.

faqs.question: Can I prevent syngamiasis while travelling?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cook all poultry thoroughly to at least 74°C, drink only treated or bottled water, eat washed produce, and wear footwear in areas heavily contaminated by bird droppings. These simple measures eliminate the main routes of infection.

faqs.question: Is eosinophilia always present with syngamiasis?
faqs.answer: Not always. Eosinophilia is reported in roughly half to two-thirds of published cases and is a useful supporting finding rather than a diagnostic requirement. A normal eosinophil count does not rule out syngamiasis when the clinical picture is otherwise consistent.

faqs.question: What happens if the worm breaks during removal?
faqs.answer: If worm fragments are left in the airway, symptoms may persist or recur. A follow-up laryngoscopy is performed to remove any remaining pieces. Anthelmintic drug therapy is added to kill any residual tissue. Complete removal in one piece is the goal.

faqs.question: Does syngamiasis affect children differently than adults?
faqs.answer: Children are at greater risk of partial airway obstruction because their larynges and tracheas are narrower. A single worm pair that causes only mild symptoms in an adult may produce stridor and respiratory distress in a toddler, making prompt diagnosis especially important in this age group.

faqs.question: Are there any long-term effects of syngamiasis?
faqs.answer: No lasting effects have been documented in the medical literature when the infection is treated. Lung function returns to normal after worm removal. There is no evidence that even prolonged untreated syngamiasis causes permanent airway scarring or immune sensitisation in adults.

faqs.question: Can animals spread syngamiasis to my family?
faqs.answer: Pet birds and infected poultry are the source of environmental larval contamination, but the infection does not spread directly from person to person or through casual contact with an infected individual. Reducing larval contamination in the home environment by proper hygiene around birds is the key preventive step.

metaDescription: Syngamiasis is a rare bird worm that infects the human throat, causing chronic cough and foreign-body sensation. Learn symptoms, diagnosis and treatment.
